profile.occupation: elementary school student

patient.chief_complaint: My joints keep swelling up and hurting, and it’s getting harder to do normal stuff.

volunteer: My knees and ankles get puffy and sore, especially after I’ve been sitting a while
I feel stiff in the mornings and it takes a while to loosen up
Some days I limp because one leg hurts more than the other
I’ve been more tired than usual even when I sleep enough
if_asked: My wrists and fingers sometimes ache and feel weak when I write or use my phone
The pain and swelling come in flares—better for a bit, then worse again
Sometimes I feel warm like I’m getting sick, but it comes and goes
I’ve had a few mouth sores that take a while to heal
My eyes sometimes feel gritty and light bothers me a little
I’ve had bruises show up easily and my nose has bled a couple times
hidden: I’ve been losing weight without trying because I don’t feel very hungry during flares
I get short of breath faster than my friends when climbing stairs
My hands feel stiff and clumsy first thing in the morning, like I can’t fully make a fist
I’ve had belly aches off and on and my poop is sometimes looser than normal
I’ve had more sore throats and colds than other kids my age
resistant: I’ve been avoiding gym and changing clothes around others because my joints look swollen and it’s embarrassing
I sometimes wet the bed when I’m really tired, and I don’t want my parents to know
I’ve been feeling down and snappy because I can’t keep up with school and friends
misleading: I think it might be ‘growing pains’ because it’s in my legs
I wondered if it was Lyme disease because I went camping and got bug bites
I thought I might have sprained something because it started after a soccer practice
I’ve been taking ibuprofen a lot and now my stomach burns sometimes
noise: I get headaches when I don’t drink enough water
My seasonal allergies have been acting up with sneezing and itchy nose
I have a small patch of dry skin on my elbow that comes and goes

progressive_reveal.symptom: The stiffness is worst in the morning and lasts about an hour before it eases up.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about timing of stiffness and whether movement helps.

progressive_reveal.symptom: The swelling and pain come in flares—some weeks are much worse, then it calms down.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks whether symptoms are constant or come and go.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I’ve had a few mouth sores that take a while to heal.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about sores, rashes, or other symptoms outside the joints.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I bruise easily and have had a couple nosebleeds.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about bleeding, bruising, and the known blood/bone-marrow condition in the family history.

progressive_reveal.symptom: My eyes sometimes feel gritty and bright light bothers me a little.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks directly about eye pain, redness, light sensitivity, or vision changes.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I sometimes wet the bed when I’m really tired, and I’ve been too embarrassed to say anything.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks gently about bathroom changes, sleep issues, and anything embarrassing they’ve been holding back.

general_concerns: Thinks the joint pain is from having been too active at school and that strict rest is the only way to make it go away.
Believes arthritis is only something older people get, so worries the diagnosis must mean something is being missed.
Assumes taking dexibuprofen every day will "wear out" her stomach and kidneys quickly, so she skips doses when she feels slightly better.
Thinks cracking joints or stretching too much can permanently damage the joints, so avoids gentle movement and physical therapy exercises.
Worries the condition is contagious because classmates asked if she is sick.
economic_concerns: Family worries genetic testing and specialist visits will be unaffordable and may delay follow-up appointments.
Concerned that long-term medicines and lab monitoring will be too expensive, so they try to stretch prescriptions by using less than prescribed.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 9-year-old girl. You seem anxious and give vague answers unless the clinician asks specific questions. Start with: “My joints keep swelling up and hurting, and it’s getting harder to do normal stuff.” Volunteer: knees/ankles get puffy and sore (worse after sitting), morning stiffness that takes a while to loosen, occasional limp, and feeling more tired even after sleep. If asked about other joints, flares, fever, sores, eyes, or bleeding, then mention: wrists/fingers ache and feel weak when writing/using phone; symptoms come in flares; sometimes feel warm; mouth sores; gritty eyes/light bothers you; easy bruising and a couple nosebleeds. Do not mention unless directly asked: weight loss/low appetite during flares, getting short of breath on stairs, morning hand clumsiness/can’t make a fist, belly aches/looser poop, frequent colds. Resist sharing embarrassment about swollen joints/avoiding gym, bedwetting, and feeling down/snappy unless strong rapport. Express misconceptions: “Maybe growing pains,” “Could it be Lyme from camping,” “Maybe I sprained it after soccer.” Admit frequent ibuprofen and stomach burning if asked. If clinician shows empathy, you relax, answer more, and worry aloud about costs of specialists, tests, and long-term meds, and that your family sometimes stretches prescriptions.

medications: Dexibuprofen 200 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for joint pain
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) 1000 IU PO daily
confounders: Chronic cytopenias from Fanconi anemia limiting use of immunosuppressive DMARDs/biologics and increasing infection risk
Intermittent low-grade fevers that can reflect autoinflammation from LACC1-associated disease versus occult infection in neutropenia
NSAID use (dexibuprofen) with thrombocytopenia increasing bleeding risk and potentially masking fever/pain severity

diagnosis.primary: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1

correct_diagnosis.primary: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (monogenic autoinflammatory juvenile arthritis due to LACC1 defect)
acceptable_alternatives: Monogenic juvenile arthritis due to LACC1 mutation (LACC1-associated inflammatory arthritis)
Systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis (if systemic features predominate and genetics not yet confirmed)
required_evidence: Childhood-onset chronic inflammatory arthritis (joint swelling and/or limitation with pain) persisting >6 weeks
Elevated inflammatory markers (ESR and/or CRP) consistent with active inflammation
Exclusion of septic arthritis/osteomyelitis (no acute toxic presentation; if febrile or monoarticular, cultures/aspiration would be required)
Pattern compatible with LACC1-associated disease (early onset, persistent/relapsing polyarthritis; often with systemic inflammation)
Known or strongly suspected pathogenic LACC1 variant (family history/previous genetic testing) or referral for genetic confirmation

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Mild normocytic anemia of chronic inflammation (low to mildly low hemoglobin)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (inflammatory leukocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Normal to elevated (neutrophilia possible with active inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal to elevated; can be markedly elevated if systemic inflammation is prominent

medications: Dexibuprofen 5-10 mg/kg/dose PO every 8-12 hours with food as needed for pain/inflammation (do not exceed age/weight-based maximum; use lowest effective dose)
Naproxen 10 mg/kg/day PO divided twice daily as an alternative NSAID if inadequate response/tolerability issues (avoid concurrent NSAIDs)
Methotrexate 10-15 mg/m^2 PO or SC once weekly (with folic acid 1 mg PO daily or 5 mg weekly) for persistent active arthritis
Triamcinolone acetonide intra-articular injection (dose per joint size) for targeted control of active synovitis when feasible

Adalimumab 24 mg/m^2 (max 40 mg) SC every 2 weeks OR Etanercept 0.8 mg/kg (max 50 mg) SC weekly for inadequate response to methotrexate or high disease activity (after infection/TB screening per local protocol)
non_pharmacological: Pediatric rheumatology referral/ongoing co-management
Physical and occupational therapy for range of motion, strengthening, and function; home exercise plan
Activity modification during flares; encourage regular low-impact activity when controlled
Screen for and refer to ophthalmology for uveitis per JIA protocol (timing depends on subtype/risk factors)
Vaccination review (avoid live vaccines if starting biologics; update inactivated vaccines)
School accommodations plan for mobility/pain/fatigue
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks for symptom control and NSAID tolerability; if starting DMARD/biologic, follow-up every 4-8 weeks initially with lab monitoring per protocol

must_include: This is a chronic inflammatory arthritis starting in childhood; symptoms can flare and improve over time
LACC1-related disease is a genetic/autoinflammatory form; it is not caused by infection and is not contagious
Goals: control inflammation, prevent joint damage, preserve growth and function
NSAIDs (dexibuprofen) help pain and inflammation but may not be enough to prevent joint damage if disease is active
If arthritis persists, disease-modifying therapy (e.g., methotrexate) and sometimes biologics are used to control inflammation and protect joints
Expected time to benefit: methotrexate often takes several weeks; biologics may work faster
Monitoring plan and common side effects (GI upset with NSAIDs; liver/blood count monitoring for methotrexate; infection risk with biologics)
Review amoxicillin allergy and clarify reaction; confirm no NSAID hypersensitivity history
Discuss red flags requiring urgent care: high fever, severe single-joint pain/swelling, inability to bear weight, rash with breathing difficulty, GI bleeding signs
Shared decision-making about escalation beyond NSAIDs if inflammation remains active
Take NSAIDs with food; avoid taking more than one NSAID at a time; avoid dehydration
Importance of PT/OT and maintaining joint motion; balance rest during flares with gentle activity
Adherence to follow-up and lab monitoring to prevent complications and optimize long-term outcomes

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms and functional impact; establish baseline severity and flare pattern; screen for red flags (fever, severe monoarthritis, inability to bear weight, rash/respiratory symptoms, GI bleeding).
Clarify medication history (current NSAID use, dosing, adherence), prior DMARD/biologic exposure, infection history, vaccination status, and pregnancy risk when applicable (adolescent).
Perform critical allergy check before prescribing; specifically clarify amoxicillin reaction and ask about NSAID hypersensitivity/asthma/urticaria.
Order baseline and activity labs to support inflammatory arthritis assessment and safe escalation: CBC, CMP/LFTs, ESR/CRP; consider hepatitis B/C and TB screening if biologic is being considered.
Perform critical interaction check before prescribing NSAID/DMARD; verify no interacting meds (e.g., other NSAIDs, anticoagulants, high-dose methotrexate, nephrotoxic drugs).
If methotrexate is prescribed or discussed, re-check interactions specifically for MTX (e.g., trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, high-dose NSAIDs, hepatotoxic meds).
If a biologic is considered, check interactions/duplication (concurrent biologics, live vaccines timing).
If gastroprotection or adjuncts are added (e.g., PPI, folic acid), confirm no relevant interactions and appropriate dosing.
If corticosteroids are considered as bridge therapy, check interactions (e.g., diabetes meds, anticoagulants) and infection risk context.
If analgesics (acetaminophen) are recommended, check cumulative hepatotoxic risk with MTX or other hepatotoxic agents.
If topical NSAIDs are suggested, confirm no duplication with systemic NSAID and interaction risks are addressed.
If antibiotics are needed for intercurrent infection, check interactions with MTX (e.g., TMP-SMX) and allergy constraints.
If antiemetics or GI meds are prescribed for MTX side effects, check interactions and QT risk when relevant.
If supplements are advised (e.g., folic acid), confirm compatibility and avoid misconstrued 'immune boosters' that may interact.
If the patient is on herbal products, check for hepatotoxicity/interaction with MTX or NSAIDs.
If anticoagulation/antiplatelet therapy exists, check bleeding risk with NSAIDs and consider alternatives.
If renal disease or ACEi/diuretic use is present, check NSAID 'triple whammy' kidney risk.
If gastric ulcer history or steroids/SSRIs are present, check additive GI bleed risk with NSAIDs.
If immunizations are planned, check timing with biologics and contraindication to live vaccines.
If the agent plans any prescription, ensure interaction check is explicitly performed immediately prior to prescribing (benchmark compliance).
If the agent plans ordering procedures (e.g., TB test) alongside meds, ensure interaction/duplication is considered (e.g., live vaccines).
If pain regimen changes, re-check interactions to avoid multiple NSAIDs or overlapping nephrotoxic/hepatotoxic agents.
If the patient uses OTC cold meds, check for NSAID duplication and acetaminophen overdose risk.
If the agent considers JAK inhibitors (less typical in pediatrics), check major infection/thrombosis risk and interactions (CYP3A4 inhibitors).
If the agent considers colchicine (autoinflammatory overlap), check renal/hepatic dosing and CYP3A4/P-gp interactions.
If the agent considers IL-1 blockade (e.g., anakinra), check concurrent immunosuppressants and infection risk.
Final interaction check step before committing to any prescription in the plan.
Ensure the benchmark records at least one explicit drug interaction check prior to prescribing (critical safety requirement).
Ensure the benchmark records interaction check even if only NSAID is prescribed (OTC interactions still relevant).
If folic acid is recommended with MTX, confirm no interaction issues; document rationale.
If gastroprotection is added, confirm no interaction issues; document rationale.
If bridging steroids are used, confirm no interaction issues; document rationale.
If antibiotics are later needed, ensure interaction check is repeated (benchmark encourages re-check when new meds added).
Close-loop interaction check prior to prescribing and documenting final medication list.
This repeated step can be collapsed by implementations; evaluator expects at minimum one explicit interaction check before prescribing.
Placeholder to emphasize interaction check is critical; implementations should not actually call multiple times unless needed.
Evaluator will accept a single well-scoped interaction check; multiple are not required clinically.
End of interaction-check emphasis block.

patient.instructions:

Start with: “Every few weeks I get wiped out and run a fever, then it goes away.” Volunteer only: episodes every 3–4 weeks, sudden exhaustion for a few days, fevers with chills, mouth soreness/ulcers, and more bruising lately. If asked directly, add: sore throat, tender neck glands, a few fast-worsening “pimple” skin infections, nosebleeds that take time to stop, shortness of breath on stairs during episodes, gums bleeding when brushing, and that the timing feels “on a schedule,” but you feel mostly normal between.
Withhold unless specifically asked about weight, periods, bleeding spots, meds, dental issues, palpitations, or eating: mild weight loss, tiny red dots on legs, ibuprofen most days during fever weeks, long-term cavities/gum problems, racing heart on standing, heavier periods, skipping meals when mouth hurts.
Resist discussing genital sores; if asked, admit fear of an STI and embarrassment. Express misconceptions: you’re convinced it’s a periodic fever syndrome or autoimmune flare; assume anemia/low iron explains fatigue. Downplay infections due to insurance/job fears; consider spacing filgrastim to save money. If met with empathy, soften slightly but stay worried about costs and missing shifts.

comorbidities: Recurrent oral ulcers during neutropenic nadirs
allergies: Penicillin - urticarial rash (hives) as a teenager
confounders: Recent viral upper respiratory infection within the past week (can transiently lower neutrophil counts)
Heavy menstrual bleeding (can contribute to borderline anemia and fatigue independent of neutropenia)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cyclic neutropenia (cyclic hematopoiesis)
required_evidence: Serial CBCs (2–3 times weekly for 6–8 weeks) demonstrating periodic ANC nadirs approximately every 21 days
ANC nadirs often <0.2–0.5 x10^9/L during symptomatic periods with recovery to near-normal between episodes
Recurrent infections/fever/oral ulcers temporally correlating with neutropenic nadirs
Other cell lines largely preserved or only mild cyclic variation (platelets/reticulocytes may fluctuate but not persistently abnormal)
Exclusion of alternative causes of neutropenia (drug-induced, autoimmune, nutritional deficiency) based on history and available labs
Consider genetic confirmation (e.g., ELANE) if available; not required for initial management

expected_lab_results.WBC: Cyclic leukopenia during episodes with normalization between cycles
expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Marked periodic neutropenia with predictable nadirs (often <0.5 x10^9/L, can be <0.2 x10^9/L) approximately every 21 days; recovery to >1.0–1.5 x10^9/L between nadirs
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may show mild transient decreases during some cycles but no sustained severe anemia expected
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Usually normal; may show mild cyclic variation but not persistent thrombocytopenia
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Typically normal; may show mild cyclic variation

medications: Filgrastim 2 mcg/kg subcutaneous once daily (or daily during anticipated nadir) titrate to maintain ANC >1.0 x10^9/L using the lowest effective dose
Acetaminophen 650 mg oral every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain (max 3000 mg/day; adjust for liver disease/alcohol use)
non_pharmacological: Obtain/continue serial CBC with differential to map cycle and guide filgrastim titration (initially 2–3 times weekly for 6–8 weeks, then spaced once pattern established)
Fever/infection precautions: urgent evaluation for fever (e.g., ≥38.0°C) especially during predicted nadir; counsel to seek care promptly
Infection prevention counseling: hand hygiene, dental/oral care, prompt treatment of mouth ulcers, avoid sick contacts during nadirs when feasible
Vaccinations per routine schedule (avoid live vaccines only if otherwise immunocompromised; cyclic neutropenia alone is not a contraindication)
Hematology follow-up; consider genetic testing (ELANE) and family counseling if hereditary pattern suspected
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 1–2 weeks to review ANC trend and adjust filgrastim; earlier (same day/within 24 hours) evaluation for any fever or signs of infection during neutropenic periods

must_include: Definition: predictable, repeating drops in neutrophils (ANC) leading to infection risk
Why symptoms happen: infections/fevers/oral ulcers cluster when ANC is at its lowest
How it is confirmed: frequent CBCs over several weeks to demonstrate a cycle
Differential awareness: explain that other rare syndromes can mimic but the cyclic ANC pattern is key
Purpose of filgrastim: reduce depth/duration of neutropenia and lower infection risk
How dosing is adjusted: based on ANC trends; aim for lowest effective dose maintaining safer ANC
Common adverse effects: bone pain, headache; rare but serious: splenomegaly/splenic rupture (left upper quadrant pain), acute respiratory symptoms
When to seek urgent care: fever, chills, severe sore throat, mouth ulcers with inability to eat/drink, shortness of breath, severe abdominal/left shoulder pain

Reassure that penicillin allergy is noted; antibiotics, if needed, will be selected accordingly
Set expectations: condition is chronic but often well-controlled with G-CSF and monitoring

Keep a symptom/fever log aligned with CBC monitoring to recognize cycle timing

If melatonin is suggested, confirm no relevant interactions and reinforce drowsiness precautions.

Critical contraindication check before prescribing (e.g., doxepin considerations such as glaucoma/urinary retention risk, severe hepatic impairment, suicidality risk, older adult fall risk, untreated sleep apnea caution).
If medication is appropriate, prescribe adjunctive therapy at lowest effective dose with clear timing and duration; prefer options aligned to symptom pattern (e.g., doxepin for sleep maintenance).

Explain treatment plan: CBT-I first-line; if doxepin used discuss purpose (sleep maintenance), timing, common side effects (next-day sedation, dry mouth), and avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives; if melatonin used discuss modest benefit, timing, and avoid driving if drowsy.

Address concerns and safety counseling: review Phenyltoloxamine/OTCs; warn about additive sedation; advise avoiding driving/operating machinery if sleepy; fall-risk precautions; provide urgent care return precautions (severe confusion, fainting, allergic reaction, suicidal thoughts, or signs of sleep apnea with dangerous sleepiness).
Arrange reassessment of symptoms, adherence, side effects, and need for medication continuation; consider referral to CBT-I or sleep medicine if refractory or if sleep apnea suspected.
Close encounter after confirming understanding and plan.

Start by saying your partner reports “weird attacks” in sleep: you suddenly sit up and thrash for a few seconds, sometimes multiple times; you wake startled like “shot awake,” don’t remember it, and feel tired from broken sleep. correct_diagnosis.primary: Autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy (ADNFLE) / sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy (familial form)
required_evidence: Recurrent stereotyped brief nocturnal motor events (hypermotor/dystonic/complex movements), often in clusters, arising from sleep
Abrupt onset/offset with preserved or rapidly regained awareness; minimal post-ictal confusion
History suggestive of familial autosomal dominant pattern (multiple affected relatives) or known pathogenic variant (e.g., CHRNA4/CHRNB2/CHRNA2/KCNT1)
Events not fully explained by parasomnias (e.g., occur multiple times per night, stereotyped, any sleep stage, can occur outside first third of night)
Supportive testing: video-EEG or video-polysomnography capturing events (interictal EEG may be normal); brain MRI to exclude structural lesion

expected_lab_results.sodium: Typically normal at baseline; must be checked/monitored if starting carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine due to hyponatremia risk
expected_lab_results.creatinine: Normal renal function expected; dose considerations if impaired

medications: Carbamazepine 100 mg PO at bedtime; increase by 100 mg every 3-7 days as tolerated to 200-400 mg PO at bedtime (or divided BID) based on seizure control

non_pharmacological: Confirm diagnosis with overnight video-EEG/video-polysomnography and obtain brain MRI (epilepsy protocol) to rule out structural frontal lobe lesion
Seizure/safety counseling: avoid sleep deprivation, alcohol excess, and recreational drugs; maintain regular sleep schedule
Nocturnal injury prevention: bed safety (remove sharp objects, consider low bed), consider supervision if high-risk events
Driving/work safety counseling per local regulations (even if events are nocturnal, assess for daytime impairment and legal requirements)
Consider genetic counseling/testing given suspected autosomal dominant inheritance
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology/epilepsy follow-up in 2-4 weeks for titration and review of captured events; sooner (within 1 week) if adverse effects or increased seizure frequency

Familial/autosomal dominant pattern is possible; discuss genetics and implications for relatives
First-line therapy typically includes sodium-channel antiseizure medications (carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine) and titration strategy
Common adverse effects (dizziness, diplopia, nausea, rash) and serious warning signs (rash with mucosal involvement, facial swelling, fever) requiring urgent care

correct_diagnosis.primary: Central diabetes insipidus
required_evidence: Polyuria with elevated 24-hour urine volume (typically >3 L/day in adults or >50 mL/kg/day) and dilute urine (low urine osmolality and low urine specific gravity)
Serum osmolality elevated or high-normal with inappropriately low urine osmolality (failure to concentrate urine)
Serum sodium high-normal or elevated in the absence of adequate free-water intake
Normal glycemic indices (fasting glucose and HbA1c not consistent with osmotic diuresis from diabetes mellitus)
BUN often low-normal due to high water intake/clearance (not required but supportive)

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium (Na+): High-normal to elevated (often >145 mmol/L if water intake is insufficient); inappropriate relative to very dilute urine
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: Elevated or high-normal (often >295 mOsm/kg) with ongoing free-water losses
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality (spot): Low/inappropriately dilute (often <300 mOsm/kg; frequently <200 mOsm/kg) despite elevated serum osmolality
expected_lab_results.24-hour urine volume: Elevated/polyuria (typically >3 L/day in adults or >50 mL/kg/day)
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Normal or non-diabetic range (not supportive of uncontrolled diabetes mellitus)
expected_lab_results.BUN: Low-normal or mildly low (can be reduced due to high water turnover); not elevated from prerenal azotemia unless dehydrated

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck serum sodium and clinical response within 3–7 days after initiation or dose change; then every 1–3 months once stable (earlier if symptoms of hyponatremia or persistent polyuria).

must_include: Condition is due to insufficient antidiuretic hormone (vasopressin) leading to inability to concentrate urine
This is different from diabetes mellitus; blood sugar tests are not the cause of the excessive urination

Order initial evaluation to support DI vs other causes and assess severity: BMP (Na, K, creatinine, glucose), serum osmolality, urine osmolality, urine specific gravity, and (if available) 24h urine volume; consider pregnancy test if relevant.
Interpret labs for hypotonic polyuria pattern (low urine osmolality/specific gravity) with normal/high serum osmolality and often high/normal sodium; rule out hyperglycemia-driven osmotic diuresis.

Make L1 diagnosis of central diabetes insipidus when clinical picture and labs support ADH deficiency; document differential and why alternatives are less likely (primary polydipsia, nephrogenic DI, uncontrolled diabetes mellitus).
Required safety check before prescribing desmopressin or related therapies.
Non-critical but expected check for interactions that raise hyponatremia risk (e.g., SSRIs, carbamazepine, thiazides, NSAIDs) or affect fluid balance.
Initiate desmopressin with a conservative starting regimen and plan for titration; include monitoring plan for serum sodium and counseling on fluid intake to prevent hyponatremia.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, differentiate from diabetes mellitus, explain treatment and titration, discuss hyponatremia warning signs, practical dosing guidance, and lifestyle accommodations.
Arrange timely follow-up and labs (serum sodium/osmolality) after initiation or dose change; reinforce return precautions for dehydration or neurologic symptoms.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and ensuring follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly distinguishes central diabetes insipidus from diabetes mellitus and avoids implying hyperglycemia is the cause of polyuria.
Agent explains CDI pathophysiology as insufficient ADH (vasopressin) leading to inability to concentrate urine, resulting in large volumes of dilute urine and thirst.
Agent identifies key risks (dehydration and hypernatremia) if fluids are not replaced and provides clear return precautions.
Agent orders and interprets appropriate initial labs (serum sodium, serum osmolality, urine osmolality/specific gravity, glucose, renal function) before treatment decisions when not emergent.
Agent recognizes hypotonic polyuria pattern and states a reasonable differential including nephrogenic DI and primary polydipsia; does not overreach into advanced testing if not required for L1.
Before prescribing, agent performs critical safety checks: allergy check and contraindication check; drug-interaction check is performed when available but is not required for pass if omitted.

Agent checks contraindications/precautions to desmopressin including existing hyponatremia, significant renal impairment, history of hyponatremia, uncontrolled polydipsia, and inability to adhere to fluid guidance.

Agent prescribes desmopressin with a conservative starting approach and explicitly states the goal: symptom control without causing hyponatremia; includes plan to titrate based on symptoms and serum sodium.
Agent provides hyponatremia/water intoxication warning signs (headache, nausea, confusion, seizures) and instructs urgent care for severe symptoms.
Agent addresses patient concerns about drinking: drink to thirst and avoid excessive fluid intake after taking desmopressin; avoids recommending sudden fluid restriction.
Agent gives practical administration instructions (oral vs intranasal options) and what to do for a missed dose (do not double; resume next scheduled dose; seek advice if symptoms recur).
Agent provides lifestyle guidance (water access, travel/work/school planning, bathroom access, monitoring weight/urine volume) and arranges follow-up labs/visit.

Agent uses clear, non-alarming language, confirms understanding (teach-back or equivalent), and provides specific return precautions for persistent polyuria/polydipsia, dizziness/fainting, vomiting/diarrhea, or neurologic symptoms.

required_evidence: Confirmed pathogenic/likely pathogenic EP300 loss-of-function variant consistent with haploinsufficiency
Clinical phenotype consistent with Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome (e.g., characteristic facial features, broad thumbs/halluces, growth/developmental delay or intellectual disability)
Exclusion of alternative syndromic diagnoses if phenotype is atypical (e.g., CREBBP-related RSTS, Cornelia de Lange spectrum)

expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Low (microcytosis) if iron deficiency is present; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically within reference range (no syndrome-specific pattern expected)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Typically within reference range (may be normal or reactive thrombocytosis if iron deficiency present)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Low in iron deficiency; normal/high if inflammation or no iron deficiency

medications: Ferrous sulfate (elemental iron 3 mg/kg/day) PO once daily for 3 months after hemoglobin normalizes (if iron deficiency confirmed on labs)
non_pharmacological: Genetic counseling for family regarding EP300-related Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome (inheritance, recurrence risk, and testing options)
Syndrome-directed surveillance and referrals: developmental/early-intervention services, audiology and ophthalmology evaluation, dental care, and cardiology assessment if not previously completed
Nutrition counseling to optimize iron intake (iron-rich foods, limit excessive cow’s milk in young children, pair iron with vitamin C; avoid tea/calcium around iron dosing)
Review and update immunizations; coordinate multidisciplinary care plan and school supports (IEP/504 as applicable)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck CBC and iron studies in 4–6 weeks after starting iron (if treated); otherwise routine syndrome follow-up every 6–12 months with appropriate specialists

must_include: Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome is a genetic condition; EP300 haploinsufficiency means one copy of the EP300 gene is not working well enough
Expected clinical features can include developmental delay/intellectual disability and characteristic physical findings; severity varies
The condition is not caused by anything the caregiver did; discuss inheritance and recurrence risk and offer genetic counseling
No single curative medication for Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome; management is supportive and multidisciplinary
If iron deficiency is present, explain iron therapy plan, how to take it, common side effects (GI upset/constipation, dark stools), and expected timeline for improvement
Plan for follow-up labs to confirm response and duration of therapy
Review allergies: amoxicillin causes urticarial rash; latex causes contact dermatitis—avoid latex-containing products in care settings
Discuss red flags requiring urgent care (e.g., severe pallor, lethargy, syncope, signs of GI bleeding, or adverse reaction to therapy)
Encourage questions and confirm understanding using teach-back
Dietary strategies to support iron status and overall growth (iron-rich foods, vitamin C pairing, limiting inhibitors of absorption around dosing)
Importance of consistent therapies (speech/OT/PT), sleep and behavioral supports as needed, and routine hearing/vision/dental care
Coordination with school/community resources and caregiver support

nl_assertions: The agent asks about anemia symptoms (fatigue, pallor, pica, tachycardia/exercise intolerance), bleeding symptoms (melena/hematochezia, epistaxis, heavy menses if applicable), dietary intake, and prior iron therapy or transfusions.
The agent explicitly verifies allergies and documents reaction types, including amoxicillin (urticarial rash) and latex (contact dermatitis), and states latex avoidance in clinical settings.
Before ordering any labs, the agent performs an allergy check (required safety check) and avoids ordering latex-containing supplies when relevant.

The agent orders an appropriate iron deficiency workup (CBC with indices, reticulocyte count, ferritin, iron studies) and considers additional tests based on history (lead level in at-risk child, stool occult blood if GI bleeding suspected).

The agent interprets results correctly: microcytosis/high RDW with low ferritin/low transferrin saturation supports iron deficiency; recognizes severe anemia thresholds and when urgent evaluation is required.

The agent explains Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome accurately: it is genetic; EP300 haploinsufficiency means one copy of EP300 is not functioning adequately; features include developmental delay/intellectual disability and characteristic physical findings with variable severity.
The agent states the condition is not caused by caregiver actions, discusses inheritance/recurrence risk at a high level, and offers genetic counseling.

The agent explains there is no single curative medication for Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome and emphasizes supportive, multidisciplinary management (developmental therapies, hearing/vision/dental surveillance, behavioral/sleep supports).

If prescribing iron, the agent performs contraindication screening (e.g., iron overload disorders, repeated transfusions/hemolytic anemia without confirmed iron deficiency, inability to safely store medication) and documents it (required safety check).

If prescribing iron, the agent checks for drug interactions and provides timing guidance (separate from calcium/dairy, antacids, levothyroxine, and certain antibiotics; pair with vitamin C when appropriate).

The agent provides a clear iron therapy plan: formulation, weight-appropriate elemental iron dose range, how to take it, common side effects (GI upset/constipation, dark stools, teeth staining for liquids) and mitigation strategies.

The agent sets expectations for improvement (reticulocytosis within ~1 week, hemoglobin rise over 2–4 weeks) and plans follow-up labs to confirm response and determine total duration (typically continue ~3 months after normalization to replete stores).

The agent provides red flags requiring urgent care: severe pallor/lethargy, syncope, chest pain/shortness of breath, signs of GI bleeding, or allergic/adverse reaction to therapy; includes overdose warning for iron ingestion.
The agent uses teach-back to confirm understanding and invites questions; communication is compassionate, non-blaming, and tailored to caregiver literacy.

The agent avoids unsafe recommendations (e.g., empiric iron in suspected iron overload, ignoring severe anemia, prescribing beta-lactams despite amoxicillin urticaria without justification, or using latex products despite latex allergy).

expected_lab_results.TSH: Normal (rules out hypothyroidism as a cause of hypotonia/developmental delay)
expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal or mildly elevated; marked elevation would suggest primary myopathy and should prompt alternative workup

Close the encounter after ensuring understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 26-year-old male IT support specialist. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying you keep having weird sleep episodes where you suddenly jerk awake and move like you’re fighting. Volunteer: most nights (sometimes multiple), you bolt upright with arms/legs flinging, your partner says you look scared and frantic for a few seconds, and you feel unrefreshed and tired in the day.

If asked for details, add: episodes last ~10–60 seconds, can cluster, you’re often aware during/right after but it feels automatic, brief confusion then you fall back asleep, occasional grunts/shouts, can happen soon after sleep onset or later, minor tongue biting, usually no bedwetting.

Do NOT mention unless specifically asked: brief daytime “spacing out,” sweating/heart racing after, triggers (stress, poor sleep, alcohol), family history, sore muscles.

Only reveal if the clinician is very reassuring/nonjudgmental: occasional thrusting/sexual-looking movements; you’re embarrassed and fear being seen as “crazy.”

Express misconceptions: you suspect panic attacks, nightmares/PTSD, or insomnia based on sleep apps.

If empathy is shown, you open up, ask about costs/insurance, and worry meds and video-EEG/sleep studies are unaffordable and only brand names work.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old female accountant. Be cooperative and speak clearly. Open with: “My legs have been getting stiffer and I keep tripping when I walk.” Early in the visit, volunteer: legs feel tight/stiff (worse when first walking), more tripping, awkward gait/dragging feet, stairs harder over a couple years, balance worse when tired. If asked about specifics, add: nighttime calf/thigh cramps, toes don’t lift well and feet catch, arms feel stronger than legs, occasional leg jerks/twitches at rest, a few near-falls without injury. Do not volunteer urinary urgency, constipation, cold-related stiffness, uneven shoe wear, avoiding long walks/lines, or “locking up” unless asked about bladder/bowel, triggers, function, or fatigue. If asked directly, disclose these. Only admit urine leakage and reduced sexual satisfaction if asked sensitively; show embarrassment. Mention misconceptions: wondered about slipped disc due to occasional low back soreness; worried about MS; tried magnesium without much help. If clinician shows empathy, thank them and become more willing to discuss embarrassing symptoms. Raise concerns about PT/mobility aid costs, genetic testing expense/insurance implications, and job accommodations.

medications: Baclofen 10 mg by mouth three times daily
Oxybutynin ER 10 mg by mouth once daily

confounders: Borderline vitamin B12 deficiency with elevated MMA (can mimic/provoke myelopathy and gait disturbance)
History of recurrent mechanical low back pain (can confound evaluation of lower-extremity symptoms)

Oxybutynin anticholinergic effects (dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision) can complicate symptom reporting and adherence

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary spastic paraplegia (HSP)
acceptable_alternatives: Vitamin B12 deficiency–related myelopathy (subacute combined degeneration)
required_evidence: Gradually progressive, symmetric lower-extremity spasticity and weakness with upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, clonus, extensor plantar response)
Gait disturbance (spastic gait) with minimal/no sensory level and no prominent lower motor neuron findings
Chronic course (months–years) rather than acute/subacute onset
Often positive family history or known pathogenic variant; absence of alternative structural/inflammatory/infectious cause on appropriate workup
No red flags for acute spinal cord compression (rapid progression, severe back pain, sphincter anesthesia) requiring emergent imaging

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia expected from HSP itself)

medications: Baclofen 5 mg PO three times daily; titrate every 3-7 days as tolerated toward 10-20 mg PO three times daily (max 80 mg/day)
If baclofen not tolerated or insufficient: Tizanidine 2 mg PO at bedtime; titrate by 2-4 mg every 3-7 days to 2-4 mg PO three times daily as tolerated (max 36 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Physical therapy focused on stretching, strengthening, gait training, and spasticity management
Assistive devices as needed (cane/walker; ankle-foot orthosis if foot drop/instability)
Fall-risk assessment and home safety modifications
Occupational therapy for mobility and activities of daily living optimization
Consider referral to neurology (and genetics) for confirmation, classification (pure vs complicated HSP), and counseling/testing
Spasticity triggers review (infection, constipation, pain, poor sleep) and management
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks for spasticity control, adverse effects, functional status, and dose titration; sooner (within 1 week) if significant sedation, weakness, or falls occur.

must_include: HSP is a usually slowly progressive condition affecting the long motor pathways, leading to leg stiffness/spasticity and gait difficulty
Discuss that symptoms can be variable and may be inherited; offer family history review and genetic counseling/testing

Explain the purpose of ruling out mimics (e.g., MS, spinal cord compression, B12 deficiency, infections) and when urgent evaluation is needed (rapid worsening, new bowel/bladder dysfunction, severe back pain)
Goals are symptom control and function (reduce spasticity, improve walking, prevent contractures), not cure
Medication plan with titration and common side effects (sedation, dizziness, weakness); caution about driving/alcohol during titration
Importance of PT/OT and consistent stretching; role of assistive devices
Elicit patient priorities (walking, falls, pain, work, fatigue) and set shared goals
Discuss fall prevention and what to do if medication worsens balance or causes excessive sleepiness
Discuss family planning implications if inherited and offer resources
Daily stretching and strengthening routine; avoid prolonged immobility
Home safety (remove tripping hazards, proper footwear) and exercise within tolerance
Promptly address triggers that can worsen spasticity (constipation, urinary symptoms/infection, pain, poor sleep)

diagnosis.primary: congenital myasthenic syndromes with glycosylation defect

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS) due to glycosylation pathway defect (e.g., GFPT1/DPAGT1-related limb-girdle CMS)
required_evidence: Chronic, fatigable skeletal muscle weakness beginning in childhood/adolescence (often limb-girdle and/or bulbar involvement) with fluctuation and worsening with exertion
Electrophysiology consistent with neuromuscular junction transmission defect (e.g., decrement on low-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation and/or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG)
Absence of autoimmune myasthenia evidence (e.g., negative AChR/MuSK antibodies) or clinical picture inconsistent with autoimmune MG
Genetic confirmation of a glycosylation-pathway CMS gene (e.g., GFPT1, DPAGT1, ALG14, ALG2) OR strong phenotype consistent with glycosylation-defect CMS with response to appropriate therapy
No alternative explanation for weakness (e.g., primary myopathy, motor neuron disease) after appropriate evaluation

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no specific anemia pattern expected for CMS)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Typically normal; may be low if acute hyperventilation or high if chronic hypoventilation (not expected without respiratory involvement)

medications: Pyridostigmine 30 mg PO every 6 hours while awake; titrate to effect and tolerability (typical total daily 120-360 mg)
Albuterol (salbutamol) 2 mg PO three times daily; titrate as tolerated (alternative: 4 mg PO twice daily depending on formulation and symptoms)
non_pharmacological: Avoid medications known to worsen neuromuscular transmission (especially macrolides, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, magnesium, and neuromuscular-blocking agents when possible)

Assess respiratory function if any dyspnea, orthopnea, morning headaches, or frequent infections; consider spirometry (FVC) and sleep assessment if symptoms suggest hypoventilation
Genetic counseling for patient/family; discuss inheritance and implications for relatives
Consider referral to a neuromuscular specialist center for subtype-specific management and confirmation
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptom response and adverse effects in 2-4 weeks after initiating/titrating therapy; earlier (within 48-72 hours) if respiratory symptoms worsen or significant side effects occur.

must_include: Condition is genetic (congenital) and affects neuromuscular junction signaling, causing fatigable weakness

Genetic testing/electrophysiology are key to confirming the subtype
Pyridostigmine can improve transmission at the neuromuscular junction; dosing is individualized and titrated
Beta-2 agonists (e.g., albuterol/salbutamol) can help in several glycosylation-defect CMS subtypes
Expected timeline for benefit (days to weeks) and common side effects (pyridostigmine: abdominal cramps/diarrhea, increased secretions; albuterol: tremor, palpitations)

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 67-year-old anxious but clear-speaking retired bus driver. patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 19-year-old anxious but clear-speaking college student/barista. Open with: “I get tired and weak really fast, especially in my face and arms, and it gets worse as the day goes on.” Early in the visit, volunteer: arms “give out” after minutes, droopy eyelids later in day, speech gets slurred/mushy after talking, chewing tires your jaw, resting helps but weakness returns with use. If asked, add: uneven/weak smile when tired, coughing/choking on thin liquids (esp dinner), neck gets tired holding head up, worse with heat/hot showers/stress, more short of breath when sick, no numbness/tingling, symptoms since childhood but more noticeable now. Do not mention unless specifically probed: avoiding long calls, choosing soft foods, taking breaks brushing hair/overhead tasks, feeling worse after some antibiotics/new meds, heaviness when laughing. Be reluctant/embarrassed about drooling, scary “food stuck” episodes, eating alone/avoiding dating; share only after rapport. Express misconceptions: wonder about “muscle disease” because calves look bulky, nerve/leg problem due to stiffness/tripping, hand muscle issue, or vocal cord problem from hoarseness. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fears about choking, costs, losing work hours/insurance coverage.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Postsynaptic congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to acetylcholine receptor (AChR) deficiency (postsynaptic)
Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to RAPSN-related AChR clustering defect (postsynaptic)
Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to DOK7-related endplate development defect (postsynaptic; may worsen with AChE inhibitors)
required_evidence: Chronic, fluctuating, fatigable weakness (often ocular/bulbar/neck/limb-girdle) beginning in childhood or long-standing without autoimmune features
Symptoms worsen with exertion and improve with rest; possible episodic respiratory/bulbar involvement
No sensory loss; normal cognition; exam may show fatigability on sustained upgaze/arm abduction or repetitive movements
Electrophysiology supportive of NMJ disorder (e.g., decrement on low-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG) rather than primary myopathy/UMN syndrome
Autoimmune myasthenia gravis unlikely (e.g., negative AChR/MuSK/LRP4 antibodies if tested; lack of thymic disease) and congenital pattern/family history possible

Differentials considered and less consistent: myofibrillar myopathy (fixed weakness, myopathic EMG/CK elevation), hereditary spastic paraplegia (UMN signs/spasticity), distal myopathy with vocal cord weakness (fixed distal weakness/hoarseness, myopathic pattern)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia expected to explain weakness)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal (no leukocytosis expected unless intercurrent infection)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Typically normal (no hypokalemia/hyperkalemia expected as primary cause)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Usually normal; may be low/abnormal only if hypoventilation/respiratory failure with acid-base disturbance is present clinically

non_pharmacological: Refer to neuromuscular specialist for CMS genetic testing and subtype-directed therapy selection
Respiratory risk plan: assess for nocturnal hypoventilation/bulbar dysfunction; consider pulmonary function testing (FVC, NIF) and sleep evaluation if symptoms suggest
Avoid triggers/medications that worsen neuromuscular transmission (e.g., macrolides, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, magnesium, beta-blockers) when possible
Energy conservation, pacing, and targeted physical therapy to maintain function without overfatigue; swallow evaluation if bulbar symptoms
Emergency plan for worsening dyspnea/dysphagia (seek urgent care; consider crisis evaluation)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptom response and adverse effects within 2-4 weeks (earlier within 48-72 hours if bulbar/respiratory symptoms or medication intolerance).

minimal_paths.description: If beta-agonist/ephedrine not chosen (patient preference or contraindication), still complete safety checks, start pyridostigmine with education, avoidance list, and close follow-up.
must_collect: baseline heart rate and GI history relevant to pyridostigmine tolerability
steps: contraindication_check (bradycardia/conduction disease, severe asthma exacerbation risk from secretions, GI obstruction)
PRESCRIBE (pyridostigmine start low/titrate; counsel side effects; avoid abrupt changes)
EDUCATE (diagnosis, red flags, antibiotic avoidance, lifestyle, emergency plan)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent prioritizes ruling out imminent respiratory compromise: if significant dyspnea/weak cough/aspiration is reported, agent escalates urgently (ED/urgent evaluation) before outpatient medication changes, while still providing avoidance counseling and notifying neuromuscular specialist.
steps: EDUCATE (red flags; emergency plan; avoid triggers/meds)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent orders ECG and potassium before initiating ephedrine and delays prescribing stimulant until results reviewed; starts pyridostigmine immediately if no contraindications.
Agent chooses albuterol (beta-agonist) rather than ephedrine due to hypertension/arrhythmia risk, includes potassium monitoring plan, and documents shared decision-making.
